sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=31, col=31).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 37, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 36 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The code crashed due to the unhashable list error, but the MOUSE action on the white 3x3 was executed. Let me check the current state and try clicking on the left green box.
Looking at the current state:
- Left green box at (30-32, 9-11) with charcoal dot (c) at (31, 10)
- Right green box at (30-32, 42-44) with white dot (W) at (31, 43)
- Left charcoal box at (32-36, 11-15) with white 3x3 interior
- Right charcoal box at (26-30, 44-48) with white 3x3 interior
I notice the left charcoal box has 15 pixels (Node 10) instead of 16. This means it's damaged/missing a pixel. Similarly, the right charcoal box has 15 pixels (Node 4) instead of 16.
Wait, let me look at the boundaries:
- Node 4 (right charcoal box): boundary=[[26, 44], [26, 48], [30, 48], [30, 45], [29, 44]]
- Node 10 (left charcoal box): boundary=[[32, 12], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11], [33, 11]]
These are 5x5 boxes with one pixel missing from the boundary, giving 15 pixels instead of 16. The missing pixel is where the green box overlaps.
For the right charcoal box at (26-30, 44-48), the boundary goes from (30, 45) to (29, 44), skipping (30, 44). This is where the green box at (30-32, 42-44) overlaps.
For the left charcoal box at (32-36, 11-15), the boundary goes from (36, 11) to (33, 11), skipping (32, 11). This is where the green box at (30-32, 9-11) overlaps.
So both charcoal boxes are damaged where the green boxes overlap. Maybe the goal is to repair both charcoal boxes by moving the green boxes away?
But wait, the left charcoal box was repaired earlier when I moved the green box UP. Let me check if the current left charcoal box is actually repaired.
Looking at Node 10: boundary=[[32, 12], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11], [33, 11]]
A complete 5x5 box would have boundary [[32, 11], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11]]. The current boundary has an extra point [33, 11] which suggests the left edge is damaged at row 32.
Actually, the green box at (30-32, 9-11) overlaps with the charcoal box at row 32, col 11. The green box's right edge is at col 11, and the charcoal box's left edge is at col 11. So they share col 11 at row 32.
Hmm, but the green box is at (30-32, 9-11), which means it occupies cols 9, 10, 11. The charcoal box is at (32-36, 11-15), which means it occupies cols 11, 12, 13, 14, 15. They overlap at col 11, rows 32.
So the left charcoal box is damaged because the green box overlaps it. Similarly, the right charcoal box is damaged because the green box overlaps it.
Maybe the goal is to have both charcoal boxes fully intact (16 pixels each)? To do that, I need to move the green boxes away from the charcoal boxes.
For the left panel:
- Green box at (30-32, 9-11)
- Charcoal box at (32-36, 11-15)
- Overlap at (32, 11)
- To fix: move green box LEFT, UP, or DOWN
For the right panel:
- Green box at (30-32, 42-44)
- Charcoal box at (26-30, 44-48)
- Overlap at (30, 44)
- To fix: move green box LEFT, DOWN, or RIGHT
Let me try moving the left green box LEFT to clear the overlap, and the right green box DOWN to clear the overlap.
But wait, I can only control one green box at a time. Which one is "active"?
Looking at the history, when I moved LEFT 6 times, the middle green box moved to the left. When I moved RIGHT 6 times, the left green box moved to the right. It seems like the "active" green box might be the one closest to the center or the leftmost one.
Let me try moving the left green box (at 30-32, 9-11) LEFT to clear the overlap with the charcoal box.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Code crashed but action executed. Let me check state and try to fix charcoal boxes
# Both charcoal boxes have 15 pixels (damaged where green boxes overlap)
# Goal hypothesis: repair both charcoal boxes by moving green boxes away

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 10:
        print(f"Charcoal box: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green box: boundary={n['boundary']}, children={n['children']}")

# Try moving LEFT to move the left green box away from the charcoal box
result = action(['LEFT'])
print(f"\nAfter LEFT: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 10:
        print(f"Charcoal box: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green box: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
